section_title: From the basic \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{N}=4$ Abelian confining dualities to the $\mathcal{N}=2$}{N=4 to N=2} Abelian dualities
content: From the basic \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{N}=4$ Abelian confining dualities to the $\mathcal{N}=2$}{N=4 to N=2} Abelian dualities
raw_latex: \chapter{From the basic \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{N}=4$ Abelian confining dualities to the $\mathcal{N}=2$}{N=4 to N=2} Abelian dualities}
following_context: In this appendix, we show how to prove the self-dualities for $U(1)_0$ with $[2,2]$ flavors, for $U(1)_{1/2}$ with $[2,1]$ flavors and $U(1)_{1}$ with $[1,1]$ flavors assuming only that $U(1)_0$ with $[1,1]$ flavors confines. Starting from the basic $\mathcal{N}=4$ Abelian mirror duality, we present the Aharony-like dualities needed to dualize the planar quivers presented in the paper. We start from the basic $\mathcal{N}=4$ Abelian mirror dual pair (Equation ) which relates a $U(1)$ gauge theory with one hypermultiplet to a free hypermultiplet.

Starting from the basic $\mathcal{N}=4$ Abelian mirror duality, we present the Aharony-like dualities needed to dualize the planar quivers presented in the paper. We start from the basic $\mathcal{N}=4$ Abelian mirror dual pair (Equation ) which relates a $U(1)$ gauge theory with one hypermultiplet to a free hypermultiplet. The operator map is shown below:

content: Self-Dual SQED
raw_latex: \subsection*{Self-Dual SQED}
preceding_context: Analogous to the $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror pair (Equation ), the fundamental $\mathcal{N}=2$ mirror pair is shown in Equation : with the operator map:
following_context: By composing the duality operation shown in Equation , one can derive mirror dual pairs of SQED with a generic number of hypermultiplets \textit{a la} Kapustin-Strassler . We provide a brief summary of the piecewise dualization procedure needed to find the mirror of $\mathcal{N}=2$ SQED with $F$ flavors . Each flavor is dualized using Equation to obtain a $U(1)^{F+1}$ with $2F$ charged fields, $F$ \textit{flippers} (and a corresponding superpotential) and $2F$ mixed BF interactions connecting the original $U(1)$ gauge group with the $2F$ new gauge factors.

content: By composing the duality operation shown in Equation , one can derive mirror dual pairs of SQED with a generic number of hypermultiplets \textit{a la} Kapustin-Strassler . We provide a brief summary of the piecewise dualization procedure needed to find the mirror of $\mathcal{N}=2$ SQED with $F$ flavors . Each flavor is dualized using Equation to obtain a $U(1)^{F+1}$ with $2F$ charged fields, $F$ \textit{flippers} (and a corresponding superpotential) and $2F$ mixed BF interactions connecting the original $U(1)$ gauge group with the $2F$ new gauge factors. At this point, the original $U(1)$ gauge group has no flavors and we can perform the functional integral over it obtaining a functional delta function. Implementing the delta reduces the number of gauge groups to $F-1$ , which is indeed the correct mirror dual. To dualize the planar quivers in this paper, we need some additional self-dualities of $\mathcal{N}=2$ SQED with $F=2$ . The starting point of this is the $\mathcal{N}=2$ self-dual ( \textit{modulo} flips) pair shown in Equation : with the operator map:

We provide a brief summary of the piecewise dualization procedure needed to find the mirror of $\mathcal{N}=2$ SQED with $F$ flavors . Each flavor is dualized using Equation to obtain a $U(1)^{F+1}$ with $2F$ charged fields, $F$ \textit{flippers} (and a corresponding superpotential) and $2F$ mixed BF interactions connecting the original $U(1)$ gauge group with the $2F$ new gauge factors. At this point, the original $U(1)$ gauge group has no flavors and we can perform the functional integral over it obtaining a functional delta function. Implementing the delta reduces the number of gauge groups to $F-1$ , which is indeed the correct mirror dual. To dualize the planar quivers in this paper, we need some additional self-dualities of $\mathcal{N}=2$ SQED with $F=2$ . section_title: An Application: Recovering the Symmetric Bifundamental Block from a Planar Abelian Quiver Gauge Theory
content: The duality between the $[N,N]$ bifundamental block and a planar quiver gauge theory is shown here.

section_title: An Application: Recovering the Symmetric Bifundamental Block from a Planar Abelian Quiver Gauge Theory
content: In Equation , it was demonstrated that $N$ free chiral fields are mirror dual to a planar quiver gauge theory. This is schematically repeated in Figure . Our aim in this section is to sequentially confine the planar quivers using only the pairs of Aharony duals discussed before. We demonstrate how to reduce the planar mirror of an $[N,N]$ bifundamental block to the planar mirror of an $[N-1, N-1]$ bifundamental block by following a sequence of columnwise Aharony dualizations here. Performing this sequence iteratively will confine the planar mirror.
raw_latex: In Equation , it was demonstrated that $N$ free chiral fields are mirror dual to a planar quiver gauge theory. This is schematically repeated in Figure . Our aim in this section is to sequentially confine the planar quivers using only the pairs of Aharony duals discussed before. We demonstrate how to reduce the planar mirror of an $[N,N]$ bifundamental block to the planar mirror of an $[N-1, N-1]$ bifundamental block by following a sequence of columnwise Aharony dualizations here. Performing this sequence iteratively will confine the planar mirror.

preceding_context: Our aim in this section is to sequentially confine the planar quivers using only the pairs of Aharony duals discussed before. We demonstrate how to reduce the planar mirror of an $[N,N]$ bifundamental block to the planar mirror of an $[N-1, N-1]$ bifundamental block by following a sequence of columnwise Aharony dualizations here. Performing this sequence iteratively will confine the planar mirror.
following_context: For clarity, we fix \( N = 3 \) . We begin with the planar mirror of the \([3,3]\) bifundamental [Footnote: We follow the same conventions for the Chern–Simons, mixed Chern–Simons, and FI interactions as before, and will only mention them explicitly when they differ from these standard assignments. The FI term associated with the gauge node of fugacity $u_{3,3}$ receives an additional contribution of $-\tfrac{iQ}{4}$ due to mixing with the $U(1)_R$ symmetry which has been suppressed for brevity.] : We now perform a sequence of local Aharony dualizations, column by column.

content: Schematic summary of the dualization sequence leading to the confinement of the $[3,3]$ symmetric block. In each frame, the columns being dualized are highlighted in violet. The detailed structure of the CS, mixed CS, and FI terms, as well as the resulting singlet fields, is discussed in the main text.

The detailed structure of the CS, mixed CS, and FI terms, as well as the resulting singlet fields, is discussed in the main text.}
following_context: For clarity, we fix \( N = 3 \) . We begin with the planar mirror of the \([3,3]\) bifundamental [Footnote: We follow the same conventions for the Chern–Simons, mixed Chern–Simons, and FI interactions as before, and will only mention them explicitly when they differ from these standard assignments. The FI term associated with the gauge node of fugacity $u_{3,3}$ receives an additional contribution of $-\tfrac{iQ}{4}$ due to mixing with the $U(1)_R$ symmetry which has been suppressed for brevity.] : We now perform a sequence of local Aharony dualizations, column by column.

content: For clarity, we fix \( N = 3 \) . We begin with the planar mirror of the \([3,3]\) bifundamental [Footnote: We follow the same conventions for the Chern–Simons, mixed Chern–Simons, and FI interactions as before, and will only mention them explicitly when they differ from these standard assignments. The FI term associated with the gauge node of fugacity $u_{3,3}$ receives an additional contribution of $-\tfrac{iQ}{4}$ due to mixing with the $U(1)_R$ symmetry which has been suppressed for brevity.] : We now perform a sequence of local Aharony dualizations, column by column. As this is our first example, we are pedantic and provide many details. We first provide a schematic summary of the same in Figure before diving into the details. \item \textbf{Column 1} : \item \textbf{Column 2} [Footnote: As before, we suppress the mixing between the topological and $U(1)_R$ symmetries in this quiver. The gauge nodes with fugacities $u_{3,3}$ and $u_{2,2}$ each receive an additional contribution of $-\tfrac{iQ}{4}$, while the node $u_{2,1}$ receives a contribution of $+\tfrac{iQ}{4}$.] : \item \textbf{Column 3} [Footnote: The gauge nodes with fugacities $u_{2,2}$ and $u_{4,2}$ receive an additional contribution of $-\tfrac{iQ}{4}$, while the gauge node with fugacity $u_{3,1}$ receives an additional contribution of $+\tfrac{iQ}{4}$ to their respective FI terms.] : Notice that we make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{3,3}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_4-X_1}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 4} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{4,2}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_5-X_1}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 5} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{5,1}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_6-X_1}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 4} : \item \textbf{Column 3} : \item \textbf{Column 2} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{2,2}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_6-X_3}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 1} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{1,1}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_6-X_2}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. We recognize the resulting quiver as the planar mirror of the \([2,2]\) bifundamental block. \item \textbf{Column 2} : \item \textbf{Column 3} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{3,2}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_4-X_2}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 4} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{4,1}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_5-X_2}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 2} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{2,1}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_4-X_3}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item We make use of the duality shown in Equation once again. The gauge node with fugacity $u_{3,1}$ confines and generates a singlet with charge $+1$ under $U(1)_{X_5-X_3}$ . Thus, we correctly recover all 9 components of the $[3,3]$ bifundamental block. Thus, we observe that the planar mirror of an \([N,N]\) bifundamental block can be systematically confined to yield the planar mirror of an \([N{-}1, N{-}1]\) bifundamental block. This is achieved by sequentially dualizing each column of the planar quiver—first from left to right, and then again from right to left.
